questions.question: What is the evidence grade for nature contact and wellbeing research?

options.text: STRONG evidence from hundreds of studies showing nature exposure reduces stress, improves mood, and restores cognitive resources
No evidence—nature contact is just a myth
Only works if you have access to wilderness areas
Evidence only applies to professional artists

questions.explanation: Nature contact research has STRONG evidence base (hundreds of studies, meta-analyses). Even brief nature exposure (20 min) reduces cortisol, lowers blood pressure, improves mood, decreases rumination. Mechanisms: Attention Restoration Theory (nature provides 'soft fascination' that replenishes cognitive resources) and Stress Reduction Theory (nature elicits physiological relaxation). Creative ecotherapy specifically has EMERGING evidence (fewer studies, but promising results combining nature + creativity).

questions.question: What is land art, and what is its primary therapeutic benefit?

options.text: Land art is professional landscape architecture requiring expensive tools
Creating temporary art from natural materials on-site (rocks, sticks, leaves) that teaches impermanence, mindfulness, and connection to place—photograph and release for non-attachment practice
You must create permanent sculptures that last forever
Only works if you're in a forest

questions.explanation: Land art: Creating temporary art from natural materials found on site (rock spirals, stick arrangements, cairns, snow/sand sculptures, nature weaving). Therapeutic benefits: (1) Ephemeral nature teaches impermanence and non-attachment (wind, rain, tide erase it), (2) Requires present-moment sensory attention (naturally mindful), (3) Connects you to place and ecosystem, (4) Arranging materials is meditative and rhythmic. Photograph if you want record, then release and let nature reclaim.

questions.question: What ethical guidelines should you follow when collecting natural materials for assemblage art?

options.text: Take whatever you want from anywhere—it's all free

Take only what's abundant, already fallen, and not ecologically critical. ✅ Collect: fallen leaves, downed branches, common shells, stones (small quantities). ❌ Avoid: living plants (unless invasive), rare species, protected areas, nesting materials. Leave No Trace: take less than 5% of what's present.
Only professional artists can collect natural materials
You should clear entire areas of materials for your art

questions.explanation: Ethical foraging principles: Take only abundant, already fallen, non-critical materials. ✅ Ethical: fallen leaves, downed branches, shed bark, common shells, small quantities of stones, abundant wildflower clippings, pinecones, driftwood. ❌ Don't collect: living plants (unless invasive on private land), rare/protected species, materials from national parks, nesting materials, ecosystem-critical items. Leave No Trace: Take less than 5% of what's present (if 100 shells, take 3-5 not 50). Permission: Use public parks (check rules) or private land with permission.

questions.question: How does contemplative nature photography differ from tourist snapshot mode?

options.text: There's no difference—all photography is the same

Tourist mode: Quick photo to prove you were there, move on. Contemplative mode: Photography as meditation—spend 5-10 min observing one subject from multiple angles, take 20+ photos, notice light/shadow/texture/patterns. Photography becomes mindfulness practice that forces present-moment attention.
Contemplative photography requires expensive professional camera
You should never take more than one photo of anything

questions.explanation: Contemplative photography approach: (1) Choose one subject (flower, tree, rock), (2) Spend 5-10 min observing from multiple angles, (3) Notice light, shadow, texture, color, patterns, (4) Take 20+ photos from different perspectives, (5) Review later and choose 1-3 that capture what you felt. This forces present-moment attention, trains observational skill, deepens connection to specific place. Photography becomes mindfulness practice, not just documentation. Phone camera is sufficient—best camera is the one you have with you.

questions.question: What are the key steps in outdoor nature journaling practice?

options.text: You must be a professional writer to nature journal

Find spot and settle in, Observe 5 min before writing (notice senses), Write what you notice (descriptive cataloging), Optional sketch (observational drawing), Write reflections/connections (feelings, memories, metaphors), Close with intention/gratitude. No writing skill required—simple lists and descriptions work.
Only journal about profound spiritual experiences in wilderness
Never write about what you see—only write about your feelings

questions.explanation: Nature journaling process: (1) Find outdoor spot where you can sit 15-30 min, (2) Observe first 5 min without writing (what you see, hear, smell, feel), (3) Write descriptive catalog of what's present (lists, paragraphs, stream-of-consciousness), (4) Optional: sketch simple drawing (forces you to look closer—no artistic skill needed), (5) Write reflections (what it evokes, connections to your life, gratitude), (6) Close noting one thing to carry with you (mark date/location for record). Purpose: Observational practice, not performance writing. Simple, plain description is perfect.

title: Environmental Art and Nature Connection - Quiz
